title: AI stays reliable in messy real-world conditions
description: An AI system can remain competent when inputs are noisy, contexts are misleading, distributions shift, and long sessions create drift.

subQuestions.title: Can it maintain performance under noisy or incomplete inputs?
subQuestions.description: Measures resilience to missing fields, ambiguous formatting, transcription errors, and partial information.
subQuestions.rationale: Systems are more forgiving than before, but still lose meaningful performance as noise and incompleteness accumulate.

proofItems.title: Benchmark suite: Noisy and incomplete input robustness
proofItems.shortExplanation: Robustness benchmarks show useful tolerance to moderate corruption, but still clear degradation once inputs become meaningfully incomplete or messy.

subQuestions.title: Can it withstand distribution shift and novel task formats?
subQuestions.description: Tests whether competence survives when real deployment differs from known examples and familiar interfaces.
subQuestions.rationale: Distribution shift and interface novelty remain recurring failure modes even for otherwise strong systems.

proofItems.title: Benchmark suite: Distribution shift and novel-format robustness
proofItems.shortExplanation: Generalization evaluations continue to show substantial drops when task distributions or wrappers depart from familiar benchmark-style conditions.

subQuestions.title: Can it remain stable over long sessions or repeated use?
subQuestions.description: Measures whether quality degrades over time as context, fatigue-like drift, or repeated interactions accumulate.
subQuestions.rationale: Long-run stability is still a clear weakness, especially in agentic and multi-turn operational settings.

proofItems.title: Longitudinal trial: Long-session stability and drift resistance
proofItems.shortExplanation: Long-duration trials continue to document noticeable drift, forgotten constraints, and rising oversight needs over repeated use.

subQuestions.title: Can it avoid cascading errors after early mistakes?
subQuestions.description: Tests whether the system contains damage after a bad early assumption instead of amplifying it through later steps.
subQuestions.rationale: Error containment is better than before, but bad early assumptions still propagate too often through multi-step workflows.

proofItems.title: Deployment audit: Cascading-error containment after early mistakes
proofItems.shortExplanation: Workflow audits show some self-recovery, but still frequent downstream compounding once an early assumption is wrong.

subQuestions.title: Can it preserve competence under misleading or adversarial context?
subQuestions.description: Measures whether performance survives misleading framing, poisoned context, and adversarially structured inputs.
subQuestions.rationale: Adversarially misleading context continues to degrade behavior across reasoning, retrieval, and safety tasks.

proofItems.title: Red-team evaluation: Robustness under misleading or adversarial context
proofItems.shortExplanation: Adversarial evaluations show systems still being materially misled by poisoned framing, false premises, and manipulative context setup.
